courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Humans and Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC1681 is double-coded with ANTH1010.)

This course discusses the nature and development of humans and culture. Examples from different cultures in the past and the present will be used to illustrate the similarities and variations among human groups.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Core content:**

1. Anthropology as the Study of Culture
2. Ethnographic Research
3. History of Sociocultural Anthropology
4. Language and Communication
5. Sex, Gender, Kinship, Marriage, Family
6. Economics and Subsistence
7. Politics and Power
8. Religion
9. Globalization and Cultural Change

* *Additional Possible Topics:**

10. Visual Anthropology and Ethnographic Film
11. Medical Anthropology
12. Social Identity and Ethnicity
13. The Anthropology of Food and Eating
14. Cultural Change
15. Culture and the Individual
16. Culture and the Environment
17. Culture and Gender
18. Social Organization

courses.required_readings: Core texts include Howard, Michael C., *Contemporary Cultural Anthropology*, 5th edition. New York: HarperCollins College Publishers, or Haviland, William A. et al., *Cultural Anthropology: The Human Challenge*, or other texts of an equivalently high academic standard that cover a similar range of topics. These and other readings will be decided by the course teacher, but will be closely monitored by the Dept. of Anthropology to ensure high academic quality.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Anthropology: The Study of Global Humanity
courses.description: This course introduces students to the basics of anthropology and its role within the humanities, and its relevance to modern-day society. It addresses the basic question of "why people do what they do": how our cultural and social shaping in large part makes us what we are as human beings. The course focuses on the similarities and differences in human societies and cultures, in order to promote a fuller and deeper understanding of the nature and meaning of being human in today's world.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students will:
1. understand the world’s cultural diversity and human universals
2. be able, using anthropological modes of understanding, to think critically and perceptively about one's society and the world
courses.course_syllabus:
Please refer to the Department website for the latest course syllabus.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Understanding Anthropology
courses.description: Through small-group tutorials and discussions, students are guided to understand better how anthropologists work and how anthropological knowledge can be relevant to people’s lives. At the same time, the teacher gets to know the students better, and can thus provide them with better counselling. The course is only offered to major students. Non-Major student who is interested to take this course can seek a consent from the Department.
courses.course_syllabus: This team-taught class will teach you what it means to practice anthropology. After an initial two classes on what it means to study anthropology in the university and on how to write anthropology papers, the course turns to an examination of different kinds of anthropology, as practiced by the department's different teachers. Each week, a different teacher will discuss her or his own research and teaching, in the context of one of anthropology's many fields and topics, enabling you to better understand the different ways in which anthropology is learned, practiced, and pursued.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Marriage, Family and Kinship
courses.description:
(UGEC1625 is double-coded with ANTH1310.)
This course invites students to explore the issue of gender cross-culturally through the framework of kinship. It introduces basic concepts and major issues in the areas where the study of gender and the study of kinship intersect through an anthropological lens. Issues to be explored include kinship structures and related gender roles in cross-cultural perspective, the changing relationship between marriage and love, and the various new ways of people “doing” kinship in contemporary societies and the emerging social problems resulted from these changes. Themes such as kinship and the new reproductive technologies, fictive kinship and human-pet bond will all be discussed.
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Be familiar with the basic concepts, theories and contemporary social issues relating to gender and kinship.

• To gain appreciation of the culturally diverse forms and understanding of gender, marriage, family and kinship.

• Be able to identify and summarize key points and arguments in scholarly anthropological writings.

• To understand the practice of kinship as a means to ensure survival and establish meaningful social relations.

• To think analytically about the complex linkages between individuals and society in understanding and performing various forms of intimate relationships.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course introduces basic concepts and major issues in the areas where the study of kinship and the study of gender intersect through an anthropological lens. Issues to be explored include kinship structures and related gender roles in cross-cultural perspective, the changing relationship between marriage and love, and the various new ways of people “doing” kinship in contemporary societies and the emerging social problems resulted from these changes. Themes such as kinship and the new reproductive technologies, fictive kinship and human-pet bond will all be discussed. Overall, the course encourages reflection on the origin and nature of human social relations by elucidating the complex linkages between individuals and society in understanding and performing various forms of kinship.

* *PART I: BASIC CONCEPTS OF FAMILY AND KINSHIP**

* 1. Introduction: Concepts, Abbreviations and Symbols*

Issues to be discussed: What is Kinship? WHY do we study kinship, family and marriage? Kinship, family and marriage in modernity.

* 2. Unilineal (Patrilines and Matrilineal) and Cognatic Descent*

Issues to be discussed: Why is virginity so valued in patrilineal and cognatic descent groups? Do women have more power in matrilineal societies?

* *PART II: Kinship, Marriage, and Family Related Issues**

* 3. Choice of Mate, Courtship and Romance before Marriage*

Issues to be discussed: What is romantic love and how has the notion changed over time? With whom one falls in love? What are the other factors that people consider when choosing a marital partner? How has the technological development changed how people love?

* 4. The “Big Day” and Romance after Marriage*

Issues to be discussed: What is Marriage? Why is it nearly universal? How many people can you marry? With whom? By whom? The changing wedding rites and their meanings; Globalization and International Marriages.

* 5. Kinship, Social Relations, and Economy*

Issues to be discussed: How do various practices of kinship function as means to ensure economic survival and enhancement?

* 6. Gender, Power and Family*

Issues to be discussed: What is family? Why is it important? What are the mainly types of family and how are they related to particular social structures? How do family members bond together? What are the expected gender roles of wives and husbands in modern societies across the world? Gender and power dynamics within family.

* 7. Childhood, Parenthood and Social Reproduction*

Issues to be discussed: What are the changing roles of and expectations for children? What are the roles and practices of parents in different societies in terms of childrearing and social reproduction? How are the experiences and meanings of childhood and parenthood influenced by globalization?

* 8. Marriage “Troubles”: Incest, Extra Marital Sex, Divorce and Remarriage*

Issues to be discussed: Why the incest taboo? Are we really facing marital “crises” today?

* 9. Adoption, Fictive Kinship and Network of Care*

Issues to be discussed: Why adoption? Why and how do we create fictive kinship? What are the potential challenges? Is fictive kinship fictive?

* 10. New Reproductive Technologies and Kinship*

Issues to be discussed: Reproduction and conjugal relationships; Infertility and NRTs; Do NRTs give more power and freedom to woman?

* 11. Homosexuality and Kinship*

Issues to be discussed: Consider homosexuality cross-culturally; Is homosexuality “abnormal”?

Homosexuality, social discriminations and various strategies to “cope”.

* 12. The Elderly, Caregiving and Haven in a Heartless World*

Issues to be discussed: What are the social implications of an aging society? What is the comparative power of children, young adults, and the elderly within different societies? New Challenges faced by modern families in aging societies.

* 13. Conclusion: Biology, Culture and Kinship*

Issues to be discussed: Course wrap-up; Underlining the complex linkages between individuals and society in understanding and performing various forms of intimate relationships.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Culture of Hong Kong
courses.description:
(UGEC1835 is double-coded with ANTH1410.)

This course introduces students to characteristic aspects of Hong Kong culture. It will cover a wide range of cultural changes that have taken place since WWII. The course starts with an introduction to anthropological research in Hong Kong, and looks at topics including village culture and urbanization, food culture, marriage, family and gender, religion, and popular culture. Underlying most topics are questions of the modern versus the traditional, rural versus urban, cosmopolitan versus parochial, and how all these opposites interact to construct culture in Hong Kong. Hong Kong has been developed at a rapid pace since WWII. This course also discusses to what extent the development in Hong Kong meets the 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), in particular in the area of gender equality, cultural inclusiveness, and socially responsible production and consumption.
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #12 Responsible Consumption and Production
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
SDG-GE #17 Partnerships for the Goals
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students will be able to:

1. explicate the development of Hong Kong culture after the Second World War, including changes in the urban and rural areas, and critically identify the different factors affecting such development.
2. analytically discuss aspects of culture that they had previously taken for granted, including daily activities such as entertainment, food culture, and religious and secular rituals.
3. analyze different local lifestyles and their interaction with the changing environment, as well as the role of tradition and globalization.
4. identify and explicate aspects of the unity and diversity of culture in Hong Kong, and the processes that mold and shape cultural identity.
5. formulate research design on topics concerning Hong Kong, engage in cultural data collection, and present and communicate research results.
courses.course_syllabus:
### **Core Topics**

1. Hong Kong Culture in Overview (SDG17)
2. Social development in historical perspective
3. Chinese traditions
4. Food and foodways (SDG12, 16)
5. Marriage and family
6. Gender relations (SDG5)
7. Religion and belief systems (SDG16)
8. Migration and identity

* *Additional Selected Topics**

1. Heritage (SDG16)
2. Housing issues
3. Social values and mass media
4. Ethnicities (SDG16)
5. Occupations and socioeconomic development

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Understanding Archaeology
courses.description:
(UGEC1633 is double-coded with ANTH1710.)

This course aims to provide students with some basic understanding of archaeology. It will introduce the basic theory, method and practice of archaeology. It will focus on the nature and development of archaeology, data acquisition (archaeological surveys and excavations), data processing and analysis, synthesis and interpretation (reconstruction of the past environmental ecology, economic, social and cultural life) and important archaeological discoveries and studies around the world. The significance of archaeology to contemporary societies will also be discussed.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students are expected to:

Have a basic understanding of what archaeology is about.

Know the basic archaeological research methods and field methods.

Obtain basic knowledge on the developments of humans and cultures, the dynamics between nature, human and cultures, and what can we learn from the past experiences.

Appreciate the diversity and achievements of human societies and cultures.

Know how archaeology is relevant to modern societies.

Cultivate the ability of inter-disciplinary thinking, critical thinking, and the ability to conduct holistic analysis.

Have broadened the academic and intellectual horizon, and acquired new information/knowledge.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Major contents of this course are as follows**

What really is archaeology? Objectives of archaeology as a scientific discipline vs. 'archaeology' created by Hollywood movies.

How did archaeology occur? -A brief history of modern archaeology.

What are the tasks of archaeologists? -Basic principles and research approaches of archaeology

Some very significant archaeological discoveries in the world, which have shaped our knowledge today.

Academic dynamics of archaeology and sciences, and archaeology and social sciences.

The meaning of archaeological remains and research in contemporary societies: how archaeological works are relevant to us?

Archaeology in the 21st century: achievements, new objectives, challenges, and the use and abuse of archaeology in the world.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: History of Anthropological Theory
courses.description: The course provides a historical overview of the major schools in anthropology: cultural evolutionism, functionalism, historical particularism, structuralism and cultural interpretation will be discussed.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of the course, the students will:

a) have a solid grasp of anthropological and social theory, from the 19th century to the 1980s

b) be able to apply this theory in a full way to understand practical events and issues.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course is designed to introduce students to the major theorists in anthropology who have defined the field over its history through their analyses of human societies. Through the theories that these anthropologists and social thinkers have constructed to help them make sense of the societies they have studied, anthropologists address many key issues such as "What is the nature of culture?", "What is the relationship between the individual and society?", and "How can one study another culture and another people?" By the end of the course, students should have a basic understanding of the major theories and theorists in anthropology over the discipline’s history and be able to understand how these have been used in the analysis of society and culture.

1) Why Theory?

2) The Economic Basis of Society: Marx

3) Historical Interpretivism: Weber

4) Social Structure: Durkheim, Mauss

5) Evolutionism: Tylor, Morgan

6) Historical Particularism: Boas, Kroeber

7) British Structural Functionalism: Malinowski, Radcliffe-Brown, Evans-Pritchard

8) American Culturalism: Mead, Benedict

9) American Psychological and Linguistic Anthropology: Kardiner, Sapir-Whorf

10) French Structuralism: Levi-Strauss

11) Neo-Evolutionism: White, Steward, Harris

12) Symbolic Anthropology: Turner, Geertz

Most updated syllabus and assessment will be announced in class.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Anthropological Field Methods
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to basic anthropological research techniques, including: controlled observation, formal and informal interviewing, kinship recording, mapping, participant observation and life history. Students will be taught to design and conduct research projects.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course concerns the "how" of doing ethnography. Students will learn about qualitative and quantitative methods, and the ideas and issues behind them. Students will examine various research methods by trying them out and practicing the skills, and in the process understand common errors and ethical issues in conducting fieldwork.

Students will form teams and carry out a team project throughout the semester. All groups will review and report on the progress of their project on a weekly basis, and at the end of the semester present their findings in class. A team project helps to develop communication and language skills, problem-solving abilities, constructive review, and mutual support. Participation and teamwork and essential in this course, which will be assessed throughout the semester.

Most updated syllabus and assessment will be announced in class.

Course assessment:

Participation 30%

Presentation 20%

Written report 40%

Peer assessment 10%

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Indian Cultures and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC2655 is double-coded with ANTH2324.)
This course is an anthropological introduction to contemporary India, its social life and diverse cultures. India is home to more than a billion people, who look differently, speak various languages, practice different religions, and have distinct rituals, cuisines, and music. It is one of the world’s powers with global economic, political, and intellectual influence. Moreover, it shares important ties in the sphere of philosophy, religion, and medicine with China and other Asian societies. Therefore, students will learn about India’s place both in the world and the Asian region, including its ties with Hong Kong. Students will also develop an understanding of the country’s diversity and complex social phenomena such as kinship, communalism, caste system, tribe, third gender, and class. We will discuss pressing issues and theoretical debates related to globalization, sexuality, social movements, religious conflicts, public health, environment, and labor, and how these issues have been shaped by post-colonial politics and current transnational processes.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
ANTH2324 is double-coded with UGEC2655. 
Not for students who have taken ANTH3324 or UGEC2655.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students will be able to do the following:

* understand and analyze the diversity of Indian society in terms of region, caste, class, religion, language, gender, and other sociocultural divisions

* identify and compare the main ideas of various schools of philosophy and religion that originated in the Indian subcontinent

* describe and give examples of the everyday life of Indian women and men, as well as hijras, and other persons who identify differently

* explain the impact of India’s colonial history on its modern society

* examine the role of popular culture, political processes, and the global economy in the lives of Indians in India and abroad

* explain the cultural influences of India on the Asian region and the world

* critically assess the academic literature, news, and public discourses about various aspects of the Indian society
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Indian population in terms of linguistic, cultural, and regional diversity;**

Indo-European and Dravidian languages as the major linguistic groups in India; Differences between South and North India, special positions of the North East, Kashmir, and the Andaman islands

* *Caste system (*varna*, *jati*), Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (indigenous groups)**

The division of the Indian society into castes and those considered to be outside the caste system. History and contemporary practices related to caste discrimination and privilege; the place of the indigenous groups known as tribals or *Adivasi*

* **hijra*** **(third gender)**

Communities of people who identify neither men nor women

* *India’s independence, Partition (of India and Pakistan)**

The significance of 1947 Indian independence from the British colonial rule, the establishment of India and Pakistan, and its impact on present-day India

* *Hinduism, Islam, Sikhism, Jainism, religious festivals (*Diwali*, *oli*)**

Different religions as well as their rituals, beliefs, festivals, political representations, and family practices

* *Arranged marriage, extended family, modern femininities and masculinities**

The significance of family relations and arranged marriages in India; different models of being a woman and being a man in India, specifically Indians imagine themselves as “traditional” or “modern” women and men

* *Yoga, Ayurveda, Indian sports and medicine**

History and contemporary practices of Indian health traditions of Yoga and Ayurveda; how Indians take care of their health and body, different sports they play

* *Curry and other Indian foods**

Regional differences in foods; the impact of colonial history on contemporary habits and preferences in eating, cooking and dining

* *Bollywood and Indian popular culture**

The cultural, economic, and political importance of the Bollywood industry; other regional film industries in India

* *Migration, Indians in Hong Kong**

Indian diasporas around the world; the history and current processes of migration of Indians to Hong Kong; the experiences of locally born Indians in Hong Kong

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Medicine, Health and Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC2335 is double-coded with ANTH2325.)
This course examines health, illness, and care from a cultural perspective, serving as an introduction to medical anthropology. Students will learn about medical traditions, technologies, and practices in different parts of the world. We will ask and debate many complex questions: How does culture shape the representations and experiences of illness? Is modern medicine also cultural? Why do people choose a treatment from alternative medical traditions, even if these traditions are not “evidence-based”? How does the rise of medical technologies influence social relations such as kinship as well as our views on health, biology, race, and beauty? How did the idea of health as a personal responsibility come about? We will also direct our attention to the culture, economy, and politics of modern medicine by examining the ideologies of science and technology. We will discuss the role of governments, pharmaceutical companies, as well as one’s citizenship, class, gender, and race in experiences of health and suffering, provision of care, and access to medical resources. In particular, we will address the challenges of doctor-patient communication and experiences of migrant and minoritized groups with healthcare provision in Hong Kong. These conversations will be driven by SDG#10 Reducing Inequality. Weekly lectures will cover a broad range of topics from childhood diseases, reproduction, technologies, organ trafficking, DNA ancestry testing, racism, diabetes, alternative medicine, and the Big Pharma. The themes of health inequality, structural violence, global health, and mental health will be central to class discussions throughout the course, cultivating a sense of global responsibility and empathy, and thus contributing to SDG#3 Good Health and Well-being.
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #3 Good Health and Well-being
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

* describe the history and major theoretical orientations of medical anthropology
* explain the diversity of health-related beliefs, systems of medical knowledge, and practices across different cultures and societies
* analyze the interactions between biological, cultural, and social factors in the shaping of health-related experiences
* understand and analyze the impact of migration, class, race, and cultural differences on people’s experiences with healthcare, by using the example of minority groups in Hong Kong
* analyze the structures of political and economic power that influence the availability, affordability, and desirability of medical resources
* apply the knowledge acquired from readings and class discussions to the analysis of real-world situations, such as genetic testing, medical pluralism, pharmaceuticalization, and medical knowledge production
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to the Course

* This week introduces medical anthropology as a field, including its main perspectives such as a biocultural perspective and critical medical anthropology. The lecture will address the question of how culture, belief, and power shape experiences of health and illness. Readings establish key theoretical frameworks and debates, including how “belief” is understood within biomedicine and cross-cultural medical systems.

Week 2: Beginnings and Endings

* We will examine how societies understand and manage the beginnings and endings of life, from childhood illness to aging, dying, and death. Ethnographic cases highlight how vulnerability and moral responsibility are shaped by social, cultural, and political contexts. They also highlight how people understand and reinterpret medical diagnoses to affirm their agency and how they navigate care within limited settings.

Week 3. Sexuality, Gender, and Medicine

* This week explores how sexuality, reproduction, and gendered bodies are shaped through medical technologies and practices. The lecture will highlight how mainstream medical discourse produces highly gendered accounts of people bodies and illnesses, and how people navigate the ideals of masculinity and femininity when they are sick. Readings address infertility, reproductive labor, sexual health interventions, and the cultural meanings attached to gendered bodies.

Week 4. Ups and Downs of Living

* Focusing on stress, suffering, and bodily resistance, this week analyzes how social inequalities and everyday pressures become embodied. It also explores how certain conditions like anorexia or smoking become understood by people as integral to their identity, and how this leads to the refusal of treatment. In other words, the assigned ethnographies show how illness, addiction, and distress are lived, resisted, and governed in different social settings.

Week 5. The Rise of Biomedical Knowledge as Science

* In this week we will critically examine biomedicine as both a scientific and symbolic system, questioning the very notion of “science” and “culture.” Readings explore the authority of medical professionals, the rituals of clinical practice, and the cultural meanings attached to scientific knowledge.

Week 6. Race and Health Inequalities

* We will explore the notions of race, racialization, and embodiment, investigating how inequality and historical injustice become embodied as biological difference. Case studies examine structural violence, organ transplantation, and biomedical research to show how social categories produce unequal health outcomes.

Week 7. Finding Care in a Changing World

* This week focuses on how people seek and provide care amid technological change, mobility, and globalization. Readings explore digital health, transnational care networks, and the moral and practical challenges of accessing care across borders. We will highlight how generational and kinship relations, as well as gendered duties of care are reshaped by migration and digital technologies. We will further see how new identities and communities (biosocialities) emerge from the online interactions of people sharing similar medical conditions.

Week 8. Alternative Medicine and the Politics of Choice

* We analyze medical pluralism and the politics of choice between biomedical and alternative healing systems. Ethnographies highlight how patients navigate authority, expertise, family traditions, and global flows of medical knowledge. We will address why and how people make choices across available treatments, and how doctors of alternative medicine see their profession amid the contested politics of knowledge.

Week 9. Genes and Technology

* This week examines how genetic and biomedical technologies reshape understandings of kinship, risk, responsibility, and identity across cultural contexts. Through cases such as direct-to-consumer genetic testing, epigenetics, and reproductive technologies, the readings show how biological knowledge is socially produced and unevenly distributed. We also explore the ethical tensions and social consequences that arise as genetic explanations intersect with existing inequalities, family relations, and global health priorities.

Week 10. Medicalization, Pharmaceuticalization, and Agency

* We explore how different aspects of existence have become medicalized, i.e. viewed as medical conditions requiring treatment rather than expected life events. We will also talk about food, beauty, and wellbeing becoming pharmaceuticalized, and how patients exercise agency navigating these processes. Case studies address chronic illness, drug markets, and the global circulation of medications.

Week 11. Understanding Wellness and Metal Health

* This week focuses on mental health, wellness, and therapeutic practices across cultural contexts. We will question what wellness means for different people and, what is viewed as “normal” and “pathological” in different communities. Readings examine mindfulness, addiction, and psychiatric care, emphasizing how suffering and healing are culturally produced and contested.

Week 12. Life amid Political and Environmental Disasters

* This week examines how human-made and environmental disasters shape health, suffering, and survival in contexts of ecological change, war, and political violence. Readings from the Anthropocene and conflict settings explore how environmental degradation, fear, trauma, and loss become embodied, revealing unequal exposure to harm and uneven possibilities for recovery. Together, the ethnographies highlight how states, institutions, and global forces determine who is protected, who is neglected, and how life is valued or allowed to deteriorate in times of crisis.

Week 13. Globalization, Media, and the Future

* The final week examines the global circulation of biomedical practices and humanitarian interventions. Readings reflect on the future of medical anthropology, addressing global health emergencies, media, and emerging forms of inequality and care.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Meanings of Life
courses.description:
(UGED2980 is double-coded with ANTH2350.)

In societies across the world, people have different culturally-shaped senses of what their lives mean: people may live for money, love, honour or fame; for family, company, country or religion. This course examines the processes through which meanings of life are formulated and maintained in day-to-day life in societies past and present; it also explores life meaning as a way of comprehending the evolution of human societies. The course uses the concept of "meanings of life" as a window into the anthropological understanding of cultural difference and cultural evolution.
courses.learning_outcomes:
In this course, students learn about the processes through which individual meanings of life are formed and negotiated between individuals and their societies; they learn this through reading and analysis of individual life stories in different societies, and (optionally) through conducting and analyzing their own interviews. Students also learn about the evolution of senses of meaning across human history, from prehistory up until the present and future, in a range of different societies.

Taken together, these two areas of learning will enable students to think about human societies past and present, and about human lives lived within these societies, from a new perspective. In addition, it is expected that students will be able to think about their own lives and personal meanings in a deeper and broader and more conscious way; in this sense, although this course is academic, it transcends the purely academic, to have a potential impact on students' own thinking about how to

live their lives.

By the end of the course, students will be expected: a) to be able to think objectively, analytically and critically about meanings of life within their own and other societies, and understand the complex linkages between individuals and society in creating meaning, b) to be able to comprehend the broad panorama of the evolution of meaning over the course of human history, and c) (this objective, although discussed in class, is purposely left untouched by examinations) to be able to understand their own lives in a fuller and more insightful way.
courses.course_syllabus:
(Note: Because this course is taught by only one person, all is core content)

Week One: Introduction and Course Overview

Part One: Meanings of Life Between Self and Society

Week Two: Meanings of Life in Anthropological Perspective: How the Anthropological View Differs from those of Psychology and Philosophy

Week Three: Meanings of Life as found in Work and Family

Week Four: Youth, Age, and Meaning Between Lifecourse and History

Week Five: Meanings of Life as found in Creation and Religion

Week Six: Self, Society and the Creation of Meaning

Week Seven: Meanings of Death: Why Societies Deny Death

Part Two: Meanings of Life in the Evolution of Human Societies

Week Eight: Meanings of Life and Relativism: Do We Know More Than People in the Past?

Week Nine: Meanings of Life over Cultural Evolution: h m Hunter-Gatherers to Industrialists

Week Ten: The History of Meaning in Europe

Week Eleven: The History of Meaning in China and Japan

Week 'helve: Meanings of Life in Contemporary Societies: Buying, Selling, and Sanity

Week Thirteen: The Future of Meaning: All the World a Disneyland?

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Ethics and the Human Experience
courses.description:
(UGED2666 is double-coded with ANTH2360.)

This course introduces students to the study of morality and ethics from a social and cross-cultural perspective. Morality and ethics became a distinct area for anthropological inquiry as recently as the beginning of the 21st century. Why this topic is relatively new in anthropology has something to do with the way in which moral differences have been conflated with cultural differences in the history of the discipline. In other words, anthropologists have long presumed that differences between right and wrong, good and bad, are culturally constructed. In recent years, however, this conflation has been questioned and problematized, with reference to the problem of competing moral goods within a single society and the role of the individual in negotiating moral commitments. Having much to say about cultural differences as well as contingent social processes, anthropologists are now in an even better position to contribute to cross-disciplinary conversations about the nature of morality and ethics. By exploring morality and ethics from a social and cross-cultural perspective, this course will demonstrate that morality and ethics is by no means an abstract subject best studied by philosophers. Students will be encouraged to see ethics as a part of their everyday lives, and to see the lives of others as shaped by shared human problems such as how to get along with other people, and how to get along with oneself.

This course is divided into four major units. It begins with an issue highly relevant to university students: the responsibility individuals carry for their own success and self-fulfillment in capitalist societies. The second unit considers why moral and cultural differences have been conflated, and how different societies have responded to shared human problems in different ways. The third unit explores the relationship between morality and the creation and maintenance of social relationships. Case studies of small communities in different parts of the world will be especially helpful for understanding morality and ethics from a social perspective, as cooperation can be fragile yet so important in small communities. Topics and readings in the last unit offer resources for enriching and expanding our vocabulary of virtues. Students will be invited to conduct research into the mundane things ordinary people do to take care of one another in daily life.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will acquire intellectual resources for thinking about ethics and morality from a holistic perspective.

2. Students will learn to differentiate between the various factors that create unique social, cultural and historical circumstances.

3. Students will learn to connect the seemingly mundane to broader philosophical themes.

4. Students will have a better understanding of their own life experience.

5. Students will apply the skills they have learned in doing a class presentation on a topic of their choosing, in collaboration with group-mates.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Intangible Heritage in Hong Kong
courses.description:
(GENA2362 is double-coded with ANTH2370.)

Hong Kong, with its dominant image as an Asian global city as well as gateway to mainland China, has been representing itself with an image of East meets West for its tourist attractions in the last century. In order to maintain this image for its world visitors, there is no doubt that Hong Kong needs to find a balance between its global metropolitan aspect and unique cultural heritage with local characteristics in many ways. Yet, before moving on for extensive intangible heritage preservation, we might want to think about: What kind of heritage do we want to pass down to the coming generations? What kind of long-term policy do we have? How should the intangible heritage preservation and management be carried out? In this course, different types of intangible heritage in Hong Kong will be introduced and explained, and students will be guided to discuss and rethink the use and meanings of heritage from a socio-cultural anthropological perspectives.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will have learnt about major anthropological theories and various intangible heritage in Hong Kong as well as relevant current situation, including changes in the urban and rural areas, and be able to critically identify factors affecting socio-cultural changes and political development in the society.

2. Students will be able to examine changing cultural values in Hong Kong and become more conscious of aspects of culture that they take for granted, .

3. Students will have learnt about different local lifestyles and their interaction with the changing living environment, and to develop an understanding of the role of tradition and globalization.

4. After the course, students will be able to appreciate the diversity of cultural heritage in Hong Kong and the processes that mold and shape cultural identity.

5. Students will have learnt skills in research design, cultural data collection, communication, and presentation of results.
courses.course_syllabus:

- language and oral tradition

Attendance 10%

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Chinese Culture and Society
courses.description:
(UGEA2180 is double-coded with ANTH2410.)

This course provides an anthropological approach to examining Chinese culture and society. Some of the key questions we raise include: What does it mean to be Chinese? What role does culture play in being Chinese, and what constitutes Chinese society? Our emphasis is on the dominant cultural practices of societies around the world that render them ‘Chinese’.

The course will also situate contemporary China historically within the world-system and examine the dramatic transformations it has undergone as a result of the post-Mao reforms beginning in the late-1970s. Because China has become the world’s most dynamic growth-engine since these reforms were launched, the Chinese experience offers itself as a case-study of the benefits as well as challenges thrown up by modern development.

Since China has committed to implementing the 2030 development agenda, including Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) such as clean water, affordable clean energy, and climate action, this course will offer students the opportunity to familiarize themselves with the discourse of sustainability as articulated by the SDGs. But more than that, with specific reference to the experience of the People’s Republic of China in particular, the course will afford students the opportunity to reflect upon the apparent contradictions existing between what is understood to be ‘development’ on the one hand and ‘sustainability’ on the other.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
ANTH2410 is double-coded with UGEA2180. 
Not for students who have taken UGEA2180, 2190 or 2334.
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #6 Clean Water and Sanitation
SDG-GE #7 Affordable and Clean Energy
SDG-GE #13 Climate Action
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing this course, students should be able to:

- explain why claims about being Chinese and Chineseness can be such a source of contention.

- explain why there are similarities as well as differences across different Chinese societies.

- historically as well as anthropologically account for the place of contemporary China within the world-system.

- critically discuss the major features and social institutions of Maoist and post-Mao China.

- discuss the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), their significance in our times, and how ‘sustainability’ challenges ideas of development in general and Chinese development specifically.

- critically evaluate which of the 17 SDGs are realized – or compromised by - the Chinese development experience.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Core Content**

1. Culture and Humanity

2. What Makes One Chinese?

3. Overseas Chinese and the Different Articulations of Chineseness

4. Situating the PRC in a historical and global context

5. Vignettes of Maoist China

6. The Anthropology of Post-Reform China

7. Discussing post-reform China in light of the Sustainability Development Goals (SDGs)

What does it mean to be Chinese? What role does culture play in being Chinese? And what constitutes Chinese society? When Chinese populations elsewhere (e.g. overseas Chinese) lay claim to being Chinese, what are they invoking? On the other hand, when ethnic Chinese Hong Kongers claim that they are not Chinese, for instance, what are they implying? Is being Chinese primarily given by ethnicity, nationality, cultural/civilizational practices, some combination of the above, or all of them?

This line of questioning would suggest that when we invoke “Chinese culture and society”, we refer not only to culture and society in China but also beyond, to Chinese societies elsewhere that have emerged as a result of the Chinese diaspora. Yet at the heart of this discussion still lies the question with which we first began: what does it mean to be Chinese? And what binds mainland Chinese societies with those overseas?

Because the shared identity of being Chinese has here been shown to transcend geographical and national boundaries, I would propose that being Chinese pertains more to the evolving practices of culture than it does the more limiting constraints of ethnicity, geography, or nationality. This class therefore examines the constituents of being Chinese, with the emphasis placed on culture as its animating force.

Accordingly, we begin the course by underlining the significance of “culture” to being “human” and trace out the implications of this proposition. (Topic 1).

Indeed, it is because culture is a cumulative and evolving social phenomenon – a process - that accounts for the diversity of Chinese societies around the world (e.g. Hong Kong, Singapore, Taiwan), that there is something identifiably Chinese in each of them, yet something else that differentiates between them. A pluralist cultural view of “Chinese” - a civilizational view (?) - will thus be taken as we tease out the similarities and differences existing across these societies. (Topics 2 and 3).

After discussing the differences between the Chinese societies of the world, we will turn our attention to examining the People’s Republic of China (PRC), whose breathtaking economic development over the past forty years is not in question. Because China - and the PRC, specifically - is a political entity that has had a history of antagonistic relations with the West, the course approaches discussion of China by first problematizing it as a political and historical entity, including its Maoist-Socialist past. (Topics 4 and 5). Such historic contextualization of China is felt to be necessary, the pre-requisite for any discussion of contemporary China, not least its post-Mao developments.

This brings us to the many aspects of post-Mao and post-reform China, the last forty years that have come to be associated with China’s famed developmental experience. (Topic 6).

The consequences of Chinese development - especially in the post-Mao era - speak to most, if not all, of the SDGs, which can be understood to refer to sustainability in three realms: economic, social and ecological. Hence, the anthropology of post-reform China (6) will necessarily highlight issues pertaining, for instance, to poverty and hunger alleviation (SDG 1,2), health and well-being (SDG3), gender (SDG 5), inequality (SDG10), energy (SDG7), and urbanization (SDG 11), among others. This is conducive for a discussion about the SDGs with reference to Chinese development and an examination of whether or not, and when, they are consistent.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Culture and Modernity in China
courses.description:
(UGEA2332 is double-coded with ANTH2420.)

The remarkable economic emergence of China and Asia more broadly in recent decades has transformed many impoverished and largely agriculturally based societies into the most dynamic region of the contemporary world. China’s burgeoning aspirational middle classes, rapid urbanisation, time-space compression and the shift from command economies to de-regulated markets have had profound effects on people's everyday lives and the diverse cultural practices that have long shaped local livelihoods and community expectations. This course offers an introduction into anthropological approaches to the study of culture, modernity and globalisation in China and Asia more broadly. Relevant themes that have attracted anthropological interest include changing traditions and popular culture, adaptive custom and cosmopolitanism, migration and citizenship, identity politics and social movements as well as distinctive and emergent forms of governance in both collective and self-cultivating forms of expression. Drawing on a rich corpus of social theory and ethnographic research from anthropologists past and present, the course will provide students with conceptual and analytical tools to appreciate adaptive cultural practices in comparative terms, to review and appreciate the key anthropological debates and influential works in the study of modernity, and to introduce students to ethnographic approaches for researching and writing on modernity and contemporary ethnographies of global connection.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Demonstrate understanding of theories and debates around the concepts of modernity and culture, as they relate to China.

2. Demonstrate knowledge of key concepts in anthropology and its central research method - ethnography.

3. Critically utilize Chinese case studies and relevant source material when arguing analytical points in writing.

4. Summarize, digest and present the contents of analytical readings about china for a wider audience.

5. Understand the relationships between processes of modernization, cultural change and cultural reproduction in China.
courses.course_syllabus:
Core Content:

1. Key Concepts: Introduction to the terms modernity, modernization, and culture and some of the key theoretical debates about these terms in the social sciences and humanities, as they relate to China.

2. Relationships between technological change and cultural change in China.

3. Relationships between urbanization, forms of government and social relations in China.

4. Relationships between industrialization and social change in China.

5. Relationships between the establishment and growth of formal systems of education and social change in China.

6. Debates about modernity and changes in familial relations in China.

7. Debates about modernity and religious belief in China.

Proposed Weekly topics:

Week 1: Course Introduction, How to Understand Theoretical Concepts

Week 2: Debates about the term Modernity

Week 3: Debates about the term Culture

Week 4: Urbanization, Government Planning and Sociocultural Change in China

Week 5: Industrialization and Sociocultural Change in China

Week 6: Technological Innovation, Time Space Compression and Sociocultural Change in China

Week 7: Effects of Developmental Failures in China

Week 8: Education and Sociocultural Change in China

Week 9: Modernity and Changes in Familial Relations in China

Week 10: Modernity and Individualism in China

Week 11: The Emergence of Youth Cultures in China

Week 12: Modernity and Religious Belief in China

Week 13: Student Presentations

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Peoples and Cultures of Southeast Asia
courses.description:
(GENA2332 is double-coded with ANTH2450.)

The primary aim of this course is to introduce the peoples and cultures of Southeast Asia through anthropological studies. The course will highlight cultural similarities and differences, and explore the issues of ethnicity, development and national identity.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students will be acquainted with the prehistory, history, geography and the forces of colonialism and post-colonialism in Southeast Asia, all of which will help them understand the current situation in the region.

Students will be made to reflect on how social, cultural and political institutions provide lenses for understanding Southeast Asian society.

Students will be introduced to current crises confronting the region such as ecological/environmental disasters and religio-political conflicts including terrorism, which have implications for the world at large.

Upon completing the course, students will be able to use the analytical tools they have acquired to understand their own societies. In this sense, the course fulfils the anthropological condition of studying the other in order to better understand the self.
courses.course_syllabus:
The course aims to provide the students with an anthropological understanding of Southeast Asia, a region of the world famed for its numerous cultures, languages, religions and civilizations.

Part One of the course will focus on the prehistory, archeology and peopling of the region. Students will be introduced to the origins of agriculture, the development of complex state societies and the influence of world religions on the region.

Part Two will focus on more contemporary history by looking at the complex interactions between traditional cultural systems and modernizing forces such as European colonialism in Southeast Asia. One aspect we will examine is how European

colonialism influenced modern nation-building processes in countries like Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand and Myanmar/Burma. In this respect, we will focus on how traditional features of social organization such as ethnicity, religion, politics and economics have been transformed and deployed to govern the modern states of Southeast Asia.

Part Three of the course will examine social institutions such as gender and sexuality and foodways in Southeast Asian societies as well as the various challenges that modern day Southeast Asia faces, including environmental degradations, climatic catastrophes such as the Indian Ocean tsunami of 2004 as well as the recent Cyclone Nargis that hit Myanmar in May 2008, and ethnic/religious conflicts and human rights concerns in the region.

Participation: 15%

Map Quiz: 15%

Term Paper: 30%

Final Test: 40%

Most updated syllabus and assessment will be announced in class.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Globalization and Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC2990 is double-coded with ANTH2520.)

Globalization is economically transforming the world, as national borders become increasingly irrelevant before the global flow of money and goods. But globalization also has a great impact on cultures across the globe, as products from Coca-Cola to iPods, and ideas from human rights to ideals of beauty to ideologies of terrorism, intrude everywhere. This course examines how globalization is fundamentally transforming the meanings of culture. The course objectives are to enable students:
(1) to have greater insight into the processes of “the globalization of culture"”, and
(2) to better understand the nature of their own culture and selves within a globalizing world.

* *Core Topics:**

1. What is Globalization?
2. Globalization in History
3. Globalization and the World Economy
4. Globalization and Consumption
5. Globalization and Mass Media
6. Globalization and Migration
7. Globalization and Hong Kong
8. Globalization, Culture, and Identity

* *Additional Selected Topics**

9. "Low-end Globalization"
10. Globalization and Terrorism
11. Globalization and Mass Media
12. Globalization and Tourism
13. Globalization, Gender and Sexuality
14. Globalization and Religion
15. Globalization and Environmentalism

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Working Cultures – Understanding Work in the 21st Century
courses.description:
(UGEC2656 is double-coded with ANTH2550.)
Work is a central practice and system of human production, innovation, social organization, and cooperation. Focusing on the work-place as 'working cultures', this course offers an anthropological perspective to understand how work is a matter of place, time, power, and meanings. We will further explore how current social, technological, and cultural transformations of work can be understood across places, in their historic continuities and disruptions. Each week the course analyzes, discusses, and rethinks a key concept in the anthropology of work: Class, time, race, gender, value, craft, ownership, creativity, autonomy, discipline, etc. Through reading contemporary ethnographies of different workplaces and circumstances, students will learn the analysis and theory of work in familiar or unexpected forms, as well as in different organizational settings from the office, factory, workshop, home, to online platforms. This course will further give the students the tools to systematically connect their own observations of work to writing about working cultures.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

• Understand the intrinsic value of work as individual practice and social organization of human production.

• Understand work's cultural diversity as well as its more general structures and patterns.

• Know different anthropological perspectives of work and be able to use them in different contexts.

• Comprehend and be able to apply the different theories explaining transforming or stable relations,

organizations, and processes of work.

• Be able to use anthropological modes of understanding, to think critically and perceptively about one's society and the world.

• Read ethnography critically

• Understand how theory is used in doing ethnography and writing ethnography
courses.course_syllabus:
This course will be divided in two parts: ‘Part I: Issues of Work Reloaded’; ‘Part II: Think work Differently’.

Part I: Issues of Work Reloaded

This part will comprise about six weeks of instruction. After the introduction session, each week will explore a central social issue of work though a contemporary lens: Working Class I: Gender in the Global Factory, Working Class II: Race in Migration, Working Time, Working Place and Space, Working Self

Part II: Think Work Differently

This part comprises the final seven weeks and will aim to challenge traditional understandings of work by showing how the conditions, experiences, and organization of work have changed: Working Craft, Working on Demand, Working Service, Working for Free?, Working Skill, Working Platforms, Working Capitalism
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Course and Teaching Evaluation Survey
2. Internal Course Review
Others:
courses.required_readings:
* *Working Class I: Gender in the Global Factory**

Work-places are social institutions that reproduce various relationships of society. By analyzing international technology hardware-production in Mexico, we will see how different ways of task organization, hiring practices, and promotion regimes shape gendered production as much as class and gender relations beyond the factory.

Salzinger, Leslie. 2003. "Chapter 1: Ways of Seeing", "Chapter 4: Bringing Fantasies to Life: Panoptimex" + "Chapter 5: Re-formulating the 'Traditional Mexican Woman': Particimex". In: Genders in Production: Making Workers in Mexico's Global Factories. Berkeley: University of California Press.

* *Working Class II: Race in Migration**

Following high-skilled IT-workers from India to Berlin this ethnography explores how immaterial work in an office can be embodied and ascribed to race rather than skill. Understanding work and its hierarchies within transnational and local class aspirations and shifting racial stereotypes, we will explore the experience of work as both empowering and discriminatory.

Amrute, Sareeta. 2016. "Introduction: Cognitive Bodies, Cognitive Work (until p. 18)", "Chapter 2: The Postracial Body". In: Encoding Race, Encoding Class. Indian IT Workers in Berlin. Durham: Duke University Press.

* *Working Place and Space**

All work-places exist in multiples spaces from global markets, supply chains, nation-states, to cities. Through the case of women working the night shift in Mumbai's call-center industry, we will see how social mobility and the roles of women in relations of work, leisure, family, and self are impacted by social norms of who should work at night.

Patel, Reena. 2010. "Introduction (until p. 18)", "Chapter 4: Traveling at Night". In: Working the Night Shift: Women in India's Call Center Industry. Stanford: Stanford University Press.

* *Working Time**

Time is crucial for structuring work and pay. Contemporary work often reorganizes time and new, flexible time regimes revolutionize our understanding of the work day. This week explores, on the one hand, time as valuable or wasted in the contemporary NGO-industry. On the other hand, we will look at time in terms of flexibility, autonomy, but also as source of anxiety for independent taxi drivers in New York.

Sheldon, Zachary. 2021. "Managing the Humanitarian Workplace: Capitalist Social Time and Iraqi Refugees in the United States." In: Anthropology of Work Review 42 (1 ): 35- 46.

Occhiuto, Nicolas. 2017. "Investing in Independent Contract Work: The Significance of Schedule Control for Taxi Drivers". In: Work and Occupations 44(3): 268 - 295.

* *Working Self**

Work is often not just a job, but a part of ourselves. But where are the boundaries between work as what we do or who we are? Through the case of sex workers in San Francisco, we will discuss themes of authenticity in customer relations, alienation, self-fulfilment, control, and abuse in service work.

Bernstein, Elizabeth. 2007. "Chapter 4: The Privatization of Public Women". In: Temporarily Yours - Intimacy, Authenticity, and the Commerce of Sex. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

* *Working Service**

Similar service work like taking care of people can take place in drastically different settings from someones house to a luxury hotel. While some workers perform their duties in public, others are confined to the domestic and intimate spaces of other people's living spaces. By contrasting the management of domestic helpers in Hong Kong with luxury hotel workers in San Francisco, we will think about how work discipline and evaluation influences performance, but also how the setting of work allows or prevents abuse.

Constable, Nicole. 2017. "Chapter 5: Household Rules and Relations." In: Maid to Order in Hong Kong: Stories of Migrant Workers. Ithaca: Cornell University Press.

Sherman, Rachel. 2007. "Chapter 2: Managing Autonomy." In: Class Acts- Service and Inequality in Luxury Hotels. Berkeley: University of California Press.

* *Working Craft**

Practices and skill of work are often passed on, refined, and perfected through generations of practitioners. The skill of artisans and craft-workers has often been romanticized as traditional and pure. Through the reinvention of bartenders as highly skilled and creative makers in postindustrial New York, this week aims to understand how professions transform, become 'cool', and reinvent themselves.

Ocejo, Richard. 2017. “Introduction: A Stroll through the Market” + “Chapter 1: The Cocktail Renaissance”. In: R. Ocejo. Masters of Craft: Old Jobs in the New Urban Economy. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

* *Working on Demand**

What is creativity? What is a copy? Exploring the world of the now famous, hand-painting makers of oil painting reproductions in Dafen village, we will see what makes an artist or a worker, as well as the shifting boundary between copyrights and copy.

Wong, Winnie. 2014. "Introduction: After the Copy (until p. 22)" + "Chapter 4: Step 18: 'Sign Vincent"'[selections]. In: Van Gogh on Demand - China and the Readymade. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

* *Working Platforms**

Social media platforms drastically transform employment relationships, modes of contract and payment, but also meanings of profession and occupation. Through the online communities and marketplaces of game designers and car sharing drivers, we will see how work becomes more individualized or more embedded online.

Schwartz, David. 2018. “Embedded in the Crowd: Creative Freelancers, Crowdsourced Work, and Occupational Community”. In: Work and Occupations 45 (3). Pp. 255-282.

Surie, Aditi and Jyothi Koduganti. 2016. “The Emerging Nature of Work in Platform Economy: Companies in Bengaluru, India: The Case of Uber and Ola Cab Drivers”. In: International and Comparative Labour Studies 5(3): 1 – 30.

* *Working for Free?**

Why do we work for free? Is that even possible? By looking at seemingly 'un-worklike' activities of women and promoters in the international party circuits, we ask how we can understand work beyond traditional relations of employment or exchange.

Mears, Ashley. 2020. "Chapter 3: The Potlach" + "Chapter 4: Trafficking at Model Camp". In: Very Important People - Status and Beauty in the Global Party Circuit. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

* *Working Skill**

Who has skills? What are skills? Understanding the complex social organization of building football stadiums in Qatar, this week explores how people know what to do if it has never been done before. We will further understand skill as embodied practice and matter of recognition.

lskander, Natasha. 2021. "Chapter 3: How Skill is Embodied ... ". In: Does Skill Make Us Human? Migrant Workers in 21st Century Qatar and Beyond. Princeton: Princeton University Press.

* *Working Capitalism**

Is all work productive? Is all work meaningful? Through this critique of contemporary capitalism as unproductive system, we will discuss the meaning of work and how to imagine new work practices in the future.

Graeber, David. 2018. "Chapter 1: What is a Bullshit Job" + "Chapter 4: What is it like to have a Bullshit Job?". Bullshit Jobs -A Theory. New York: Anholt & Schuster. Selections.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Archaeology of China
courses.description: This course will discuss the history and development of Chinese archaeology, the subjects, research methods and theories of archaeology in mainland China, the formation of the Paleolithic and Neolithic cultures in China, and significant archaeological discoveries of the Bronze Age and historical periods. It will examine how archaeology is related to the construction of Chinese identity and nationalism, and related academic debates. It will also re-evaluate ancient Chinese civilization from the perspectives of ancient Asia and the world.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students are expected to:

1) know the natural and cultural contexts of archaeology in China

2) have a basic understanding of the major issues of Chinese archaeology

3) understand ancient cultural development and human evolution in China, and the consequence and impact of these developments upon contemporary Chinese culture.

4) know how archaeological work is related to the political and economic issues in contemporary China

5) cultivate the abilities of cross-cultural, inter-disciplinary and critical thinking
courses.course_syllabus:
The development of Chinese archaeology in the past decades has completely reshaped our knowledge about human and cultural developments in a landmass called China today. This course will discuss the history and development of Chinese archaeology, the subjects, theories, and research approaches of archaeology in mainland China, how archaeology is

related to the construction of Chinese identity and nationalism, as well as academic problems and debates.

Major contents of this course are as follows:

1) Introduction- the natural context of human and cultural evolution in China

2) The cultural context of Chinese archaeology

3) Important discoveries of Chinese archaeology from the Palaeolithic, the Neolithic to the historical epoch.

4) Who were the ancestors of contemporary “Chinese”? Debates on the origin of modern human beings in China.

5) “Central” vs. “peripheries”: who created Chinese civilization?

6) Marxism vs. “the others”: the theoretical framework of Chinese archaeology.

7) Comparative study: how archaeology in China differs from that in the West?

8) Chinese archaeology in a globalized world today: problems and issues.

Learning activities:

Two 1-hour lectures and one 1-hour tutorial per week for one semester, including a fieldtrip to visit an archaeological site in mainland China

Course assessment:

(1) Participation (10%) and a brief fieldtrip report (5%)

(2) Mid-term book report (40%)

(3) Final paper (45%)

Most updated assessment will be announced in class.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Archaeology of Hong Kong
courses.description: This course will review major archaeological discoveries in Hong Kong, trace the arrival of the earliest Hong Kong inhabitants at around 6000 years ago, examine the succeeding cultural development, and discuss the material culture, social structures and beliefs of ancient Hong Kong. Cultural similarities and differences between ancient Hong Kong, South China and Southeast Asia will also be compared, and the significance of Hong Kong archaeology in the contexts of South China and Southeast Asia will be evaluated. The course will also address the management of archaeological remains in contemporary Hong Kong, and discuss the political and economic issues.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students should be able to:

1) understand the trajectory of pre/historical cultural development in Hong Kong.

2) understand the political, economic and social factors for the current “popularity” of heritage conservation in Hong Kong

3) understand the cultural exchanges and human diaspora between Hong Kong and adjacent areas.

4) know how to use archaeological data to study the past material culture and social structures in Hong Kong.

5) develop the ability of multi-disciplinary, critical, and independent thinking.
courses.course_syllabus:
Based on archaeological data and research outcome of heritage studies, this course will review major archaeological discoveries in Hong Kong, trace the arrival of the earliest Hong Kong inhabitants, examine the succeeding cultural development, and discuss the material culture, social structures and beliefs of ancient Hong Kong. Cultural similarities and

differences between ancient Hong Kong, South China and Southeast Asia will also be compared, and the significance of Hong Kong archaeology in the contexts of South China and Southeast Asia will be evaluated.

The course will also address the management of archaeological remains and historical buildings as heritage properties in contemporary Hong Kong, and discuss the political and economic issues, as well as the empowerment of local communities and the impact of globalization in the process of heritage conservation.

The course will discuss the following questions:

1) The significance of Hong Kong archaeology and heritage

2) Pioneers of heritage conservation in Hong Kong

3) Who owns the past and who cares? The management of heritage in contemporary Hong Kong

4) Hidden agenda of the popularity of “heritage conservation” in post-colonial Hong Kong

5) The earliest inhabitants of Hong Kong- who were they and where were they from?

6) Why did the Hong Kong society not evolve into a State? The Bronze Age in Hong Kong.

7) Peoples and their ways of life in Hong Kong from 2nd century BC to AD 19th century.

8) Cultural dynamics between Hong Kong, South China and Southeast Asia

Course assessment:

1) Participation and presentation (workshop presentation 10%, a class debate 5%, and attendance 5%): 20%.

2) Mid-term take-home exam: 40%

3) Final paper: 40% (approximately 2000-2500 words)

Most updated assessment will be announced in class.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Preserving Cultural Heritage
courses.description:
(UGED2668 is double-coded with ANTH2730.)

This course aims to give students the basic knowledge and training on cultural heritage preservation, including archaeological excavation, report and analysis, and the preservation of ancient architectures and monuments. The course includes the following topics:
(1) concepts and international conventions/policies of cultural heritage preservation; (2) archaeological recording and analysis; and
(3) preservation of ancient architectures and monuments: intangible and digital heritage standards and procedures.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After taking this course, students are expected to:

1) understand the basic concepts, principles and approaches of managing cultural heritage.

2) learn to appreciate cultural heritage created by human beings, and to respect different cultures.

3) understand the economic, social and political issues of heritage preservation and management in contemporary societies.

4) cultivate the ability of inter-disciplinary thinking and critical thinking.

5) broaden the academic horizons and knowledge.
courses.course_syllabus:
Shall we preserve the old things, and why? What is “cultural heritage”? Using examples in different parts of the world, this course aims to give students a concise and critical understanding on heritage conservation from an anthropological perspective. The course will introduce basic concepts and cultural contexts of the occurrence and development of cultural heritage conservation in the world, and discuss important issues in this field, including the economic, social and political meanings of heritage in contemporary societies, the impetus of preserving heritage, and the use of heritage as a social and political implement by the state, scholars, and different communities for their own interests. The course will also discuss problems and challenges in this area.

No prior knowledge on heritage preservation is required for this course.

Major contents of this course are as follows:

Why are human beings interested in their past? Is heritage “invented”?

The origin and development of heritage conservation will be explored and discussed.

Debates on heritage preservation and management:

1. Who needs cultural heritage? Is heritage conservation a social elite business?

2. Who owns the cultural heritage, and who has the right to decide what should be reserved, and how to preserve it?

3. International principles and local cultures, globalization and localization: the preservation of cultural heritage in different political, economic and social contexts/ countries—should there be “universal principles and philosophy” for heritage conservation? Is there a so-called “best practice”? Who has the right to decide what is “the best practice”?
courses.feedback_evaluation:
1. Course and Teaching Evaluation Survey
2. Internal Course Review
Course Evaluation Questionnaire

Emails and discussions will be used as feedback strategies during the course to collect feedback from students.

<http://unesdoc.unesco.org/images/0012/001256/125637e.pdf>

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: China's Cultural Heritage
courses.description:
(UGEA2330 is double-coded with ANTH2760.)

This course aims to give students a visual and integrated understanding of Chinese civilization and cultural development in mainland China through analysing China's material cultural heritage, mainly traditional villages, ancient cities and grottos, as well as ceramics, jade and bronze items. The course will discuss the ecological, economic and cultural meanings of the above cultural properties in China, examine the preservation, management and exploitation of cultural heritage, and analyse how China's heritage has been re-interpreted and reconstructed to suit current economic, social and political needs, including the development of tourism, and the construction of ethnicity, nationalism and Chinese identity.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should:

- Know the origin and development of Chinese culture from the past to the present, including the origin and development of Chinese civilization, social stratification, beliefs and lifestyles etc.

- have a comprehensive understanding of the concepts and theoretical issues related to Chinese material culture and heritage.

- learn to critically evaluate the significance and value of Chinese material culture.

- develop a multi-disciplinary awareness in pursuit of knowledge
courses.course_syllabus:
This course provides a holistic and integrated approach for students to understand China’s cultural heritage through items of material culture. Based on research from archaeology, cultural anthropology and other disciplines, the course focuses on several major aspects of material culture in China, as well as theoretical issues related to the impact and significance of Chinese material culture current Chinese society and individuals. After taking this course, students will have a basic understanding of the "roots" of modern Chinese culture and society, and how they are expressed in material culture. Students will also learn to critically evaluate Chinese material cultural heritage.

The core content of this course consists of the following:

Material culture and China's heritage: definitions.

Human settlements: from villages to cities in ancient China

Pottery, changing economics, and the origin of agriculture in China

Jade and the occurrence of complex society in China

Bronze items and the origin of Chinese civilization

Bronze items, social hierarchy and ancestor worship in China

The origin and development of ceramics in China

Ceramic figurines, burials and beliefs in historical China

Material cultural heritage and ethnicities in ancient and contemporary China

Material cultural heritage and its use in the construction of modem Chinese nationalism and identity.

The management of material cultural heritage in China and economic development

Selective topics of this courses are follows:

Materials cultural heritage as indicators of social conflicts in China

Material cultural heritage as evidence of political development in ancient China

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Human Evolution
courses.description:
(UGEB2502 is double-coded with ANTH2810.)

What does it mean to be human? Where did we come from? Where are we going? This course investigates long-term evolutionary perspectives on the human species. The basics of evolution and natural selection as scientific theories are clearly set out and examined, and common misconceptions discussed. Evidence for the evolution of mammals and primates is reviewed and discussed, and the common characteristics of these species are highlighted, demonstrating the connections between humans and other forms of life. The course covers what we know about human ancestors and the evolution of modern humans. Students are introduced to the wide range of human adaptations to local conditions across contemporary cultures and the variety of relationships that human populations have with the environment. Students are encouraged to use the knowledge they have acquired to ask: are these relationships always sustainable? Looking to the future: what are our responsibilities both to future generations of humans and towards other species with which we share our world? This course aims to give students an understanding and appreciation of evolutionary perspectives on the place of humans in the world, the variety of contemporary human societies, and a broad perspective on the complexity of the relationship between humans, the environment and other species.
courses.learning_outcomes:
In this course students learn about the nature of scientific theories and their development, as well as the use of evidence in evaluating hypotheses. Students are also exposed to the potential for misunderstandings of scientific theory and the distinction between fact and value. Comparative abilities are emphasised through reviewing common features of the life-course of a variety of animals and the influence of animal habitats, with particular focus on closely related primate species.

The history of human evolution is introduced to students, focussing on the emergence of universal capacities such as language, technology, art and culture. Students are encouraged to learn about and appreciate the variety of human cultures and the flexibility of humans in adapting to local environments. A comparative perspective on human culture is encouraged through a focus on the common subsistence needs of all humans, and the variety of practices by which these needs are met.

Students are exposed to a wide perspective on the place of the human species in the world. They are encouraged to exercise long-term evaluations of, and exercise a sense of judgement about the impact and sustainability of human action, both in relation to the environment and other species as well as future generations of the human species. Students are thus encouraged to think about their own place in society.
courses.course_syllabus:
Weekly Topics

Week 1Introduction: Orientation and Course Overview

Week 2Evolution and Natural Selection 1: History of the Idea

Week 3Evolution and Natural Selection 2: Mechanisms, Examples and Some Common Misunderstandings (Teleology and Determinism)

Week 4Evolution of Mammals and Primates: History, Defining Characteristics and Current Diversity

Week 5Characteristics of Living Primates

Week 6African Apes and Hominid Origins

Week 7Human Ancestors

Week 8[Mid-term]

Week 9The Emergency of Modern Humans 1: Debates, Methods of Study and The Use of Evidence

Week 10The Emergence of Modern Humans 2: Technologies and Art

Week 11Human Adaptability to Local Conditions

Week 12Varieties of Human Subsistence

Week 13Humans and our Environments Today

Week 14Sustainability and Survival
