courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Forensic Anthropology
courses.description: Interest in the field of forensic anthropology has been heightened in the last decade through the success of forensic-themed television shows and popular novels. This course will introduce students to the field of forensic anthropology – a scientific discipline that involves the application of methods from biological anthropology and archaeology to the identification, recovery, and analysis of skeletal remains from crime scenes, mass disasters, and unexplained death. Through lectures and in-class assignments, students will learn about the methods for recognizing and recovering evidence that allows for the determination of time since death, manner and cause of death, and the identification of individuals.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will:

* Have a foundational understanding of basic skeletal biology, and are able to identify bones and specific features of various skeletal elements of the adult and subadult human skeletons.
* Learn about how osteological methods can be used to create biological profiles, and help identify possible cause and manner of death.
* Gain new perspectives into the strengths and weaknesses of standard forensic anthropology methods.
* Understand the ethical issues that arise when working with human remains from archaeological, humanitarian, and medico-legal contexts.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to forensic anthropology

Week 2: Human osteology

Week 3: Estimating time since death

Week 4: Sex determination

Week 5: Age determination

Week 6: Stature determination

Week 7: Ancestry determination

Week 8: Cause and manner of death – part 1

Week 9: Cause and manner of death – part 2

Week 10: Ante-Mortem Skeletal conditions

Week 11: Post-Mortem Skeletal conditions

Week 12: Lab day (field trip)

Week 13: Overview and summary
courses.required_readings:
Textbook: Byers, SN (2017) Introduction to Forensic Anthropology. Routledge.

<https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/cuhk-ebooks/detail.action?docID=4694526>

* *Week 1:** Introduction, establishing Medico-legal significance, and how to recover human remains in forensic scenario: textbook chapters 1, 3, 4, and

Cattaneo 2007 – Forensic Anthropology: Developments of a Classical Discipline in the New Millennium. *Forensic Science International* 165(2-3): 185-193.

* *Week 2:** Basic human osteology: textbook chapter 2, and

Rissech 2021 – The Importance of Human Anatomy in Forensic Anthropology. *European Journal of Anatomy* 25(S2): 1-18.

* *Week 3:** Estimating post-mortem interval and initial examination of the remains: textbook chapters 5,6, and

Wescott 2018 – Recent Advances in Forensic Anthropology: Decomposition Research. *Forensic Sciences Research* 3(4): 278-293.

* *Week 4**: Sex determination: textbook chapter 8, and

Krishan, Chatterjee, Kanchan, Kaur, Baryah, & Singh 2016 – A Review of Sex Estimation Techniques during Examination of Skeletal Remains in Forensic Anthropology Casework. *Forensic Science International* 261:162.e1-165.e8

* *Week 5:** Age determination: textbook chapter 9, and

Ubelaker & Khosrowshahi 2019 – Estimation of Age in Forensic Anthropology: Historical Perspective and Recent Methodological Advances. *Forensic Sciences Research* 4(1): 1-9

* *Week 6:** Stature determination: textbook chapter 10, and

Krishan, Kanchan, Menezes, & Ghosh 2012 – Forensic Anthropology Casework – Essential Methodological Considerations in Stature Estimation. *Journal of Forensic Nursing* 8(1): 45-50.

* *Week 7:** Ancestry determination: textbook chapter 7, and

Dunn, Spiros, Kamnikar, Plemons, & Hefner 2020 – Ancestry Estimation in Forensic Anthropology: A Review. WIRES Forensic Science 2(4): e1369

* *Week 8:** Cause and manner of death (part 1): textbook chapters 11, 12, and

Baraybar & Gasior 2006 - Forensic Anthropology and the Most Probable Cause of Death in Cases of Violations Against International Humanitarian Law: An Example from Bosnia and Herzegovina. *Journal of Forensic Sciences* 51(1): 103-108

* *Week 9:** Cause and manner of death (part 2): textbook chapters 13, 14, and

Pinheiro, Cunha, Symes 2015 – Over-interpretation of Bone Injuries and Implications for Cause and Manner of Death. *In:* Skeletal Trauma Analysis: Case Studies in Context*.* Passalacqua, Rainwater (eds.) Wiley Pp. 27 – 41.

* *Week 10:** Ante-mortem skeletal conditions: textbook chapter 15, and

Cunha 2006 – Pathology as a Factor of Personal Identity in Forensic Anthropology. *In:* Forensic Anthropology and Medicine: Complementary Sciences from Recovery to Cause of Death. Schmitt, Cunha, and Pinheiro (eds.) Totowa: Humana Press Inc. Pp. 333 – 358

* *Week 11:** Post-mortem skeletal conditions: textbook chapter 16, and

Blau 2016 – How Traumatic: A Review of the Role of the Forensic Anthropologist in the Examination and Interpretation of Skeletal Trauma. Australian Journal of Forensic Sciences 49 (3): 261-280.

* *Week 12**: Additional aspects of individualisation and how to obtain a positive identification: textbook chapters 17, 18, and

Quatrehomme, Fronty, Sapanet, Grévin, Bailet, Ollier 1996 – Identification by Frontal Sinus Pattern in Forenisc Anthropology 83(2): 147 – 153.

Christensen 2005 – Testing the reliability of frontal sinuses in positive identification. Journal of Forensic Sciences 50(1): 1 – 5.

* *Week 13:** Conclusion and summary: textbook chapter 19, and

Dirkmaat, Cabo, Ousley, & Symes 2008 – New Perspectives in Forensic Anthropology. *American Journal of Physical Anthropology* 137 (S47): 33-52

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Seminars in Anthropological Theory
courses.description: The course is designed to give M.Phil and Ph.D. students a solid foundation for understanding sociocultural anthropological theory. It provides an advanced review of major theoretical approaches, from classic theories of Marx, Weber, and Durkheim, through twentieth-century theories of Boas, Radcliffe-Brown, Levi-Strauss, and Geertz, to contemporary theories such as those of Bourdieu, Foucault, Ortner, and Appadurai.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil Anthropology or PhD Anthropology
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will

1) have a solid grasp of anthropological and social theory, from the 19th century to the present, and

2) be able to apply this theory in a full way to your own prospective research.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course is designed to give M.Phil and Ph.D. students a solid foundation for understanding sociocultural anthropological theory. The readings are sometimes long, but they are all important in coming to understand anthropological theory, in all its

complexities. By the end of the class, you should a) have a solid grasp of anthropological and social theory, from the 19th century to the present, and b) be able to apply this theory in a full way to your own prospective research.

Assessment:

1) A 10-12 page paper on the history of theory (40% of grade)

2) A 16-18 page paper discussing theory in the context of students’ own coming research (50% of grade)

3) Class participation (10% of grade).
courses.required_readings:
Introduction: Why Anthropological Theory?

- -Robert H. Lavenda and Emily A. Schultz, “Theory in Cultural Anthropology,” in Core Concepts in Cultural Anthropology (Mountain View CA: Mayfield, 2000), p. 185-203.

- -Thomas Hylland Eriksen, “Theories,” in What Is Anthropology? (London: Pluto 2004), p. 61-81.

Marx and Weber

- -Karl Marx, “The German Ideology,” in Robert Tucker, ed., The Marx-Engels Reader (New York: W. W. Norton, 1978), p. 146-175.

- -Max Weber, “Introduction,” “Asceticism and the Spirit of Capitalism,” in The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism (New York: Charles Scribners’ Sons, 1958), p. 13-31, 155-183.

- -E. C. Cuff, W. W. Sharrock, and D. W. Francis, “Karl Marx, Max Weber” in Perspectives in Sociology, (London: Routledge, fourth edition, 1998), p. 15-20, 23-32, 41-50, 56-59.

Durkheim and Mauss

- -Emile Durkheim, “The Elementary Forms of the Religious Life,” in W. Lessa and E. Vogt, eds., Reader in Comparative Religion: An Anthropological Approach (New York: Harper and Row, 1979), p. 27-35.

- -Emile Durkheim, “The Evolution of Morality,” in Robert N. Bellah, ed., Emile Durkheim On Morality and Society (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1973), p.63-146

- -E. C. Cuff, W. W. Sharrock, and D. W. Francis, “Emile Durkheim,” in Perspectives in Sociology, (London: Routledge, fourth edition, 1998), p. 64-71, 75-79.

- -Mauss, Marcel, The Gift: Forms and Functions of Exchange in Archaic Societies (New York: Norton, 1967), “Introductory,” Chapter 1, and Chapter 2, pp. 1-45.

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Durkheim,” “Mauss” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 46-59, 121-133

- -Background Reading: Thomas Hylland Eriksen, “Four Founding Fathers,” A History of Anthropology (London: Pluto Press, 2001), p. 36-53

The Birth of Modern Anthropology

- -Edward Burnett Tylor, “The Science of Culture”; Lewis Henry Morgan, “Ethnical Periods”; Franz Boas, “The Methods of Ethnology”; Bronislaw Malinowski, “The Subject, Method and Scope of this Inquiry.” In Paul Erickson and Liam Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006, second edition), p. 29-50, 99-105, 171-186

- -Richard J. Perry, “The Idea of Evolution,” “The Idea of Relativism,” in Five Concepts in Anthropological Thinking (Upper Saddle River NJ: Prentice-Hall, 2003), p. 12-54, 159-188.

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Tylor,” “Morgan,” “Boas,” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 5-45

British Social Anthropology

- -A. R. Radcliffe-Brown, “On Joking Relationships,” “On Social Structure,” in Structure and Function in Primitive Society (New York: Free Press, 1965), p. 90-104, 188-204.

- -E. E. Evans-Pritchard, “Introduction,” The Nuer, (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1940), p. 1-15

- -E. E. Evans-Pritchard, “Witchcraft Explains Unfortunate Events,” in William A. Lessa and Evan Z. Vogt, eds., Reader in Comparative Religion: An Anthropological Approach (Harper and Row, 1979), p. 362-366

- -Meyer Fortes and E. E. Evans-Pritchard, “Introduction,” African Political Systems,

Max Gluckman, “Rituals of Rebellion in Southeast Africa,” In Paul Erickson and Liam Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006, second edition), p. 187-221

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Malinowski,” “Radcliffe-Brown,” “Evans-Pritchard,” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 134-173

- -Background Reading: L.L. Langness, The Study of Culture, (Novato, CA: Chandler & Sharp, revised edition, 1987), p. 74-98

American Cultural Anthropology

- -Robert Lowie, “Conclusion,” Primitive Society, Alfred Louis Kroeber, “What Anthropology is About,” Margaret Mead, “Introduction,” Coming of Age on Samoa, Ruth Benedict, “The Individual and the Pattern of Culture,” Edward Sapir, “The Unconscious Patterning of Behavior in Society,” in Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006, second edition), p. 106-143, 247-258

- -Benjamin Lee Whorf, “The Relation of Habitual Thought and Behavior to Language.”

In Alessandro Duranti, ed., Linguistic Anthropology: A Reader (Oxford: Blackwell, 2001),

p. 363-381

- -Richard J. Perry, “The Idea of Culture,” in Five Concepts in Anthropological Thinking (Upper Saddle River NJ: Prentice-Hall, 2003), p. 55-94.

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Kroeber, “Benedict,” “Sapir, “Mead,” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 65-116

- -Background Reading: L.L. Langness, The Study of Culture, (Novato, CA: Chandler & Sharp, revised edition, 1987), p. 99-108.

French Structuralism

- -Edmund Leach, “Structuralism in Social Anthropology,” in P. Erickson and L. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Broadview, 2006), p. 152-164

- -Claude Levi-Strauss, “The Structural Study of Myth,” in Structural Anthropology (New York: Anchor Doubleday, 1967), p. 202-228. GN320.L453

- -Sherry Ortner, “Is Female to Male as Nature Is to Culture?” in Woman, Culture, and Society, M. Z. Rosaldo and L. Lamphere, eds. (Stanford: Stanford University Press, 1974), p. 67-87. HQ1154.R68

- -Richard J. Perry, “The Idea of Structure,” in Five Concepts in Anthropological Thinking (Upper Saddle River NJ: Prentice-Hall, 2003), p. 95-132.

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Levi-Strauss,” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 231-246

- -Background Reading: Edmund Leach, Claude Levi-Strauss (London: Fontana, 1970)

The Turn to Interpretation

- -Victor Turner, “Symbols in Ndembu Ritual,” Clifford Geertz, “Thick Description: Toward an Interpretive Theory of Culture,” in Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006, second edition), p. 299-336

- - “Ritual and Social Change: A Javanese Example,” “Deep Play: Notes on the Balinese Cockfight,” in The Interpretation of Cultures (New York: Basic Books, 1973), p.142-170, 412-454. GN315.G36

- -“Clifford Geertz: Unabsolute Truths,” New York Times Magazine, April 9, 1995, p. 44-47

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Turner,” “Geertz,” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 247-269

Postmodernism and Critical Advocacy,

- -James Clifford, “Introduction: Partial Truths,” from Writing Culture, in Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006), p. 422-444

- -Renato Rosaldo, “The Erosion of Classic Norms,” in Culture and Truth (Boston: Beacon, 1989), p. 25-44.

- -Philip Carl Salzman, “Critical Advocacy: Feminism and Postmodernism,” “Reflections on Anthropological Theory,” Understanding Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theory (Prospect Heights IL: Waveland, 2001), p. 113-125, 127-142

- -“Objectivity and Militancy: A Debate,” articles by Roy Andrade and Nancy Scheper-Hughes; commentary. Current Anthropology , vol. 36, no. 3 (1995): 399-441.

Environmental Determinism / Biology

- -Leslie White, “Energy and the Evolution of Culture,” in P. Bohannan and M. Glazer, eds, High Points in Anthropology, 2d edition (New York, Alfred A. Knopf, 1988), p. 337-355.

- -Marvin Harris, “India’s Sacred Cow,” in James Spradley and David McCurdy, eds., Conformity and Conflict (New York, HarperCollins, eighth edition, 1994), p. 130-139.

- -E.O. Wilson, “The Morality of the Gene,” [from Sociobiology: The New Synthesis] in R. Jon McGee and Richard L.Warms, eds., Anthropological Theory: An Introductory History (Mountain View, CA: Mayfield, 2000) p. 395-400. GN33.M33 2000

- -Marshall Sahlins, “Introduction,” “Critique of the New Sociobiology,” The Use and Abuse of Biology, in Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006), p. 289-298.

- -Lee Cronk, “Preface,” “The Blob,” “First Contact,” “An Infectious Idea, in That Complex Whole: Culture and The Evolution of Human Behavior (Boulder CO: Westview, 1999), p. ix-xv, 39-88.

Culture and Globalization

- -Robert H. Lavenda and Emily A. Schultz, “Globalization and the Culture of Capitalism,” Core Concepts in Cultural Anthropology (Mountain View CA: Mayfield, 2000), p. 169-183.

- -Sidney Mintz, “Introduction,” “Power,” “Eating and Being,” in Sweetness and Power (London: Penguin, 1985), p. xv-xxx, 151-214.

- -Ulf Hannerz, “The Local and the Global: Continuity and Change,” “The Global Ecumene as a Landscape of Modernity,” “The Withering Away of the Nation?” in Transnational Connections (London: Routledge, 1996), p. 17-29, 44-55, 81-90.

- -Arjun Appadurai, “Disjuncture and Difference in the Global Cultural Economy,” in Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006), p. 514-524

- -Gordon Mathews, “Chungking Mansions: A Center of ‘Low-End Globalization.’” Ethnology XLVI (2): 169-183

Culture, Self, and Self-Presentation

- -Clifford Geertz, “From the Native’s Point of View”: On the Nature of Anthropological Understanding,” in Local Knowledge: Further Essays in Interpretive Anthropology (New York: Basic Books, 1983), p. 55-70.

- -Claudia Strauss and Naomi Quinn, “A Cognitive/Cultural Anthropology,” in Robert Borofsky. ed., Assessing Cultural Anthropology (New York : McGraw-Hill, 1994), p. 284-297.

- -Erving Goffman, “Performances,” in The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life (London: Penguin, 1959), p. 28-82.

- -Erving Goffman, “On Face-Work,” in Interaction Ritual (New York: Pantheon, 1967), p. 5-45.

- -Gerald Berreman, “Prologue: Behind Many Masks: Ethnography and Impression Management,” in Hindus of the Himalayas (Delhi: Oxford University Press, 1993), xvii-lvii.

Culture, Practice, and Power: How We Shape the Society That Shapes Us

- -Sherry Ortner, “Theory in Anthropology Since the Sixties,” in Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy, eds., Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory (Peterborough CA: Broadview, 2006), p. 453-485.

- -Pierre Bourdieu, “Structures and the Habitus,” in Outline of a Theory of Practice (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1977), p. 72-95.

- -Richard Jenkins, Pierre Bourdieu (London: Routledge, 1992), p. 66-102. H4477.F8J46 1992

- -Sherry Ortner, “Updating Practice Theory,” “Power and Projects: Reflections on Agency,” in Anthropology and Social Theory (Durham NC: Duke University Press, 2006), 1-18, 129-153

- -Background Reading: Jerry D. Moore, “Ortner,” “Bourdieu,” in Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (Walnut Creek, CA: Altamira Press, 2004), p. 303-337.

Why We Can’t Comprehend the World

- -Karl Marx, “The Fetishism of Commodities,” in R. Tucker, ed., The Marx-Engels Reader (New York: W. W. Norton, 1978), p. 319-329.

- -Michel Foucault, “The Body of the Condemned,” “Docile Bodies,” “The Means of Correct Training,” “Panopticism,” “Complete and Austere Institutions,” “Illegalities and Delinquency,” in Discipline and Punish, reprinted in P. Rabinow, ed. The Foucault Reader (New York: Pantheon, 1984), p. 170-238.

- -William Gibson, “And What Should We Do Next, O Google?” The New York Times, Sept. 2, 2010

- -Ernest Becker, “Self-Esteem,” “Culture: The Relativity of Hero-Systems,” in The Birth and Death of Meaning (New York: The Free Press, 1971), p. 65-74, 112-129.

- -Roy Baumeister, “Epilogue,” in Meanings of Life (New York: Guilford Press, 1991), p. 357-370.

Cultural Identity: Who Are We, Anyway?

- -Frederick Barth, “Introduction.” Ethnic Groups and Boundaries: The Social Organization of Culture Difference. ( Boston: Little, Brown, 1969), p. 9-38

- -Stuart Hall, “The Question of Cultural Identity,” in S. Hall, D. Held, and T. McGrew, eds., Modernity and its Futures (Cambridge, UK: Polity, 1992), p. 274-316.

- -Akhil Gupta and James Ferguson, “Beyond ‘Culture’: Space, Identity, and the Politics of Difference,” in Gupta and Ferguson, eds., Culture, Power, Place: Explorations in Critical Anthropology (Duke: Duke University Press, 1997), p.33-51

- -Gordon Mathews, “On the Meanings of Culture,” “Searching for Home in the Cultural Supermarket,” in Global Culture / Individual Identity: Searching for Home in the Cultural Supermarket (London: Routledge, 2000), p. 1-23, p. 177-197.
courses.recommended_readings: Two of the books listed in the syllabus, Readings for a History of Anthropological Theory edited by Paul A. Erickson and Liam D. Murphy (2006), and Jerry D. Moore’s Visions of Culture: An Introduction to Anthropological Theories and Theorists (2004) are available in the campus bookstore.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Seminars in Research Methods
courses.description: The course aims to train and develop students’ ability to do anthropological research. Course contents consist of class lectures and independent research projects, and focus on field methods, data analysis and presentation, and thesis writing.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil Anthropology or PhD Anthropology
courses.course_syllabus:
(I) Introduction: "Methods Imply Theory"
(II) Research Design: The Proposal
(III) Ethics
(IV) Unit of Analysis and Sampling
(V) Interviewing Skills
(VI) Questionnaires
(VII) Cultural Domain Analysis and Scales
(VIII) Participant Observation, key informants, rapport
(IX) Keeping and Retrieving Field Records and Notes
(X) Direct and Unobtrusive Observation
(XI) Text Analysis, Documents, and Working with Assistants
(XII) Qualitative Data Analysis (Models and Matrices) and Quantitative Methods
(XIII) Analysis and Writing
courses.required_readings:
1) Bernard, H. Russell. 2006. Research Methods in Anthropology, 4th edition. Alta Mira Press.
2) Ng, Pedro Pak-tao. 2003. Effective Writing: A Guide for Social Science Students. The Chinese University Press.
courses.recommended_readings: Available in class and on Moodle.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Directed Studies
courses.description: For furthering a student’s studies, anthropological methods and theories will be introduced to him/her in terms of his/her special area and a research paper will be written under the supervision of a teacher.
courses.learning_outcomes: Student will learn anthropological methods and theories in terms of his/her special area and a research paper will be written under the supervision of a teacher.
courses.course_syllabus: Student will read books and articles on an anthropological topic negotiated between student and teacher.
courses.required_readings: Student will read books and articles on an anthropological topic negotiated between student and teacher.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Directed Studies
courses.description: For furthering a student’s studies, anthropological methods and theories will be introduced to him/her in terms of his/her special area and a research paper will be written under the supervision of a teacher.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in PhD in Anthropology
courses.learning_outcomes: Student will learn anthropological methods and theories in terms of his/her special area and a research paper will be written under the supervision of a teacher.
courses.course_syllabus: Student will read books and articles on an anthropological topic negotiated between student and teacher.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Feedback from PhD student during the course
Reflections of teachers/internal examiners of thesis committee
Exit interviews with students after completion of degree
courses.required_readings: Reading lists are created for each student in the first week of the course depending on the topic and needs of the student.
courses.recommended_readings: please see above required readings.

courses.subject: ANTH
courses.title: Thesis in Progress
courses.description: The student must write a thesis with the permission of and under the supervision of an instructor. The thesis shall embody the results of his/her research which are substantially an original contribution to anthropological knowledge.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil Anthropology or PhD Anthropology
courses.learning_outcomes: Student will write a thesis with the permission of and under the supervision of an instructor.
courses.course_syllabus: Student will write thesis chapters under the supervision of an instructor.
courses.required_readings: Books and articles related to thesis.
